title: Capstone Plugin Plan
prompt: Create a complete implementation plan for a WordPress plugin that exposes a public read-only REST endpoint and a private settings screen for cached external API data. Include architecture, storage, permissions, nonces/auth, caching, cron, UI, tests, and release checks.
hints: This is the final workshop. A passing answer should be implementable by another engineer.

model_answer.summary: Plan the plugin as a WordPress-native system with an explicit public read surface, private settings workflow, bounded caching, and a release gate.
model_answer.work: Architecture: keep a small bootstrap with plugin headers, constants, autoloading, activation/deactivation hooks, and namespaced service classes for settings, API client, cache, cron, REST, admin UI, and diagnostics. Storage: use options for private settings, transients or object cache for external API responses, and a schema-versioned custom table only if cached records need high-volume querying. Public REST: expose GET /cached-data with a permission_callback that allows safe read-only data and never reveals API keys or raw upstream payloads. Private UI: build a Settings API screen guarded by a capability such as manage_options, verify a nonce on saves, validate and sanitize settings, and escape output. Caching and cron: fetch upstream data with the WordPress HTTP API, timeout/error handling, cache keys, TTL, stale-safe fallback policy, and a scheduled refresh that is unscheduled on deactivation. Diagnostics: expose bounded status such as cache age, last refresh, schema version, and next cron run without secrets. Tests: cover services with PHPUnit, REST permissions, admin nonce/capability paths, cron scheduling, cache hit/miss, and manual browser smoke. Release: run php -l, PHPCS/WPCS, Plugin Check, readme/privacy/external service review, compatibility checks, and final ZIP install test.
checks: Verify public REST responses are read-only and secret-free.
Test private settings save for capability, nonce, validation, sanitization, and escaping.
Test cache hit, cache miss, timeout, bad status, and stale fallback behavior.
Verify cron scheduling on activation and unscheduling on deactivation.
Run php -l, PHPUnit, REST integration tests, browser smoke, PHPCS/WPCS, Plugin Check, and release readme/privacy review.
model_answer.why_this_is_good: It is implementable by another engineer because each subsystem has a WordPress API, a boundary, and a verification path.
common_misses: A public endpoint that leaks private upstream settings.
No nonce or capability plan for the settings screen.
Caching described vaguely with no timeout or failure policy.
No deactivation cleanup for scheduled jobs.
Release checks treated as an afterthought.
model_answer.revision_prompt: Revise by turning each subsystem into a decision: API, data ownership, permission boundary, failure mode, and test.

summary.description: One or two sentences explaining the answer.

work.description: The main implementation, review, or design answer.

checks.description: Verification steps, review checks, or acceptance criteria.
